text: Hello, this is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English.
zh: 大家好,欢迎收听BBC英语教学的《六分钟英语》节目。

text: I'm Pippa.
zh: 我是皮帕。

text: And I'm Beth.
zh: 我是贝丝。

text: In English, what we say and what we mean are sometimes two very different things.
zh: 在英语中,我们所说的话和我们真正的意思有时是两回事。

text: We often use exaggeration for emphasis or for humour – something known as hyperbole.
zh: 我们常常使用夸张手法来强调或制造幽默效果,这种手法叫做hyperbole(夸张)。

text: For example, people say, I am so hungry I could eat a horse.
zh: 比如,人们会说,我饿得能吃下一匹马。

text: But of course, no one really could.
zh: 但当然,谁也真吃不下一匹马。

text: Here's presenter Michael Rosen and linguist Derek Bousfield discussing one example of hyperbole on BBC Radio 4's Word of Mouth.
zh: 下面是主持人迈克尔·罗森和语言学家德里克·鲍斯菲尔德在BBC第四台《口耳相传》节目中讨论夸张手法的一个例子。

text: People do say absolute nightmare.
zh: 人们确实会说“绝对是场噩梦”。

text: Yeah, or as my daughters would say, I've had a mare today.
zh: 是啊,或者像我女儿说的,“我今天简直太惨了”。

text: And when they say mare, which is obviously shortened for nightmare, what it basically means is they've had a mild and potentially slightly humorous occurrence at work.
zh: 当她们说“太惨”——显然是“噩梦”的简称——时,意思基本上是说她们在工作中遇到了一件挺轻微、甚至可能有点好笑的小插曲。

text: You might call an unpleasant or difficult experience a nightmare.
zh: 你可能会把一段不愉快或艰难的经历称为“噩梦”。

text: Derek's daughter used the word mare, short for nightmare, to describe her terrible day at work.
zh: 德里克的女儿用“mare”这个词,也就是“nightmare”的简称,来形容她糟糕的一天工作经历。

text: But she was exaggerating.
zh: 但她其实是在夸张。

text: Her day wasn't really terrible, more like strange or annoying.
zh: 她那天其实并没有那么糟,更多的是有点奇怪或让人烦。

text: Can you remember a time when what you said wasn't really what you meant, Beth?
zh: 贝丝,你还记得有没有哪次你说的话其实不是你真正想表达的意思?

text: Well, sometimes we say, hi, you all right?
zh: 嗯,有时候我们会说,“嗨,你还好吗?”

text: Which means, hi, are you all right?
zh: 意思是,“嗨,你没事吧?”

text: Like, are you okay?
zh: 就像是在问,“你还好吗?”

text: but actually we don't really want to hear the answer.
zh: 但其实我们并不真的想听到答案。

text: It just means hello, doesn't it?
zh: 这句话其实就相当于“你好”,对吧?

text: Yeah, I have a friend who actually just says, alright, and they just mean hello.
zh: 是的,我有个朋友其实就只说“alright”,他们的意思就是“你好”。

text: They don't mean, are you okay?
zh: 他们并不是真的在问“你还好吗?”

text: They don't want to hear about my day.
zh: 他们并不想听我讲我这一天过得怎样。

text: No, if you're having a bad day, they don't want to know.
zh: 是啊,就算你那天过得很糟糕,他们也不想知道。

text: They're just saying hello.
zh: 他们只是打个招呼而已。

text: In this episode, we'll be finding out more about how the meaning of words changes in English.
zh: 在这一集里,我们将进一步探讨英语单词的含义是如何变化的。

text: As usual, we'll be learning some useful new words and phrases.
zh: 像往常一样,我们也会学习一些实用的新单词和短语。

text: and remember you'll find all the vocabulary from this episode on our website bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: 记得到我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com查看本期节目的所有词汇。

text: But first, I have a question for you Beth.
zh: 不过首先,贝丝,我有个问题要问你。

text: The word hyperbole comes from two ancient Greek words, hupa and balo, but what is their literal meaning?
zh: hyperbole这个词源自两个古希腊词——hupa和ballo,但它们的字面意思是什么呢?

text: Is it a to overthrow, b to overspeak or c to overthink?
zh: 是A:推翻,B:过度言说,还是C:过度思考?

text: think?
zh: 思考?

text: I have no idea, Pippa.
zh: 我完全不知道,皮帕。

text: I'm going to say to over -speak.
zh: 我猜是“过度言说”。

text: OK, we'll find out the answer at the end of the programme.
zh: 好的,我们会在节目最后揭晓答案。

text: It's not just hyperbole that changes the meaning of words.
zh: 不仅仅是夸张手法会改变词义。

text: Its opposite, something called understatement, has the same effect too.
zh: 它的反面——所谓的“轻描淡写”(understatement)——也有同样的效果。

text: Understatement involves de -emphasising something.
zh: 轻描淡写指的是弱化某件事情的严重程度。

text: For example, imagine you were served an awful meal in a restaurant, but instead Instead of saying how bad the food was, you just said it was less than perfect.
zh: 比如,假设你在餐厅吃到一顿很难吃的饭菜,但你没有说食物有多糟糕,而是说“味道不算完美”。

text: Here's linguist Derek Bousfield discussing understatement on BBC Radio 4's Word of Mouth.
zh: 下面是语言学家德里克·鲍斯菲尔德在BBC第四台《口耳相传》节目中讨论轻描淡写的内容。

text: We love understatement as a way of kind of signalling that something much more serious has gone on.
zh: 我们喜欢用轻描淡写的方式来暗示其实发生了更严重的事情。

text: It's a kind of performative, sardonic, not taking oneself too seriously identity when you do that.
zh: 这是一种表演性的、带点讽刺意味的态度,表示自己不把事情看得太重。

text: Because, you know, taking yourself too seriously is a no -no in British culture.
zh: 因为你知道,在英国文化中,把自己看得太重要是绝对不行的。

text: Yes.
zh: 是的。

text: And so linguistically not taking yourself too seriously is really the done thing.
zh: 所以从语言上说,不把自己太当回事才是真正“该做的事”。

text: Understatement is valued in British culture, unlike taking yourself too seriously.
zh: 轻描淡写在英国文化中备受推崇,而把自己看得太重则不然。

text: If you take yourself too seriously, you think you're more important than you really are.
zh: 如果你把自己看得太重,就意味着你觉得自己比实际更重要。

text: In Britain, this is a no -no, a way of behaving that is not socially acceptable.
zh: 在英国,这是一种“no-no”,也就是社会上不被接受的行为方式。

text: In other words, it's not the done thing, meaning it's not what you're expected to do in a particular social situation.
zh: 换句话说,这不是“the done thing”,意思是在特定社交场合中不被期待去做的事。

text: Another area where the meaning of words can be easily misunderstood is apologising.
zh: 另一个容易被误解词义的场合就是道歉。

text: Here, Michael Rosen responds to an imaginary situation where his toe gets accidentally stepped on by Derek Bousfield for BBC Radio 4 programme Word of Mouth.
zh: 接下来,迈克尔·罗森在BBC第四台《口耳相传》节目中回应了一个假设情境——德里克·鲍斯菲尔德不小心踩到了他的脚趾。

text: Imagine I stepped on your toe and I said, oh Michael, I'm really sorry, and you said, it's all good.
zh: 假设我踩到了你的脚趾,然后说,“哦,迈克尔,真的很抱歉”,而你说,“没事没事”。

text: Oh, right.
zh: 哦,是这样啊。

text: That probably means it isn't.
zh: 那大概意味着其实并不是“没事”。

text: Yes, I'm actually a bit miffed, a little bit annoyed but then I don't want to have a barney with you, you're a stranger, so I don't want to get into a whole thing.
zh: 是的,我其实有点不高兴,有点小恼火,但我又不想跟你吵起来,你是个陌生人嘛,我不想把事情闹大。

text: So I say, yeah, it's all good.
zh: 所以我就说,“嗯,没事没事”。

text: It's all good, exactly.
zh: 没事没事,就是这样。

text: It's a bit like, I mean that's a British English one and an equivalent American English one would be no worries.
zh: 这有点像……我是说那是英式英语的说法,对应的美式英语说法就是“no worries”。

text: If someone tells you it's all good, they could actually be feeling miffed, an adjective meaning slightly annoyed or hurt by someone's behaviour.
zh: 如果有人告诉你“it's all good”(都挺好),他们其实可能感到有点miffed——这个形容词的意思是因为某人的行为而略微生气或受伤。

text: But no matter how annoyed you are, you probably don't want to get into a barney, a loud argument or quarrel.
zh: 但不管你有多恼火,你大概都不想卷入一场barney,也就是激烈的争吵。

text: So you say something like, it's all good or no worries and move on.
zh: 所以你会说类似“没事没事”或者“不用担心”这样的话,然后就翻篇了。

text: As an English learner, unclear word meanings are a challenge.
zh: 作为英语学习者,不明确的词义确实是个挑战。

text: A lot depends on the situation and on the relationship between speaker and listener.
zh: 很多时候取决于具体情境以及说话者和听话者之间的关系。

text: So don't be shy, get talking to others in English.
zh: 所以别害羞,大胆用英语和别人交流吧。

text: And if you make a mistake, say no worries and carry on.
zh: 如果你说错了,就说“没关系”,然后继续说下去。

text: OK Pippa, I think it's time to reveal the answer to your question.
zh: 好了,皮帕,我想是时候揭晓你那个问题的答案了。

text: Yes, you thought the ancient Greek words hooper and ballo meant over -speak, but the answer was actually a – to overthrow.
zh: 是的,你之前以为古希腊词hupa和ballo的意思是“过度言说”,但正确答案其实是A——“推翻”。

text: Hooper means beyond and ballo means I throw.
zh: hupa的意思是“超越”,ballo的意思是“我投掷”。

text: Oh well.
zh: 哦,原来如此。

text: OK, let's recap the vocabulary we've learnt, starting with mare, short for nightmare, meaning an unpleasant or difficult experience.
zh: 好了,我们来回顾一下今天学到的词汇,首先是mare,是nightmare的简称,意思是不愉快或艰难的经历。

text: experience.
zh: 经历。

text: If you take yourself too seriously, you consider yourself and your actions more important than they really are.
zh: 如果你把自己看得太重,就是说你认为自己和自己的行为比实际更重要。

text: In Britain, this is a no -no, a way of behaving that is not socially acceptable.
zh: 在英国,这是一种“no-no”,也就是社会上不被接受的行为方式。

text: The done thing, on the other hand, is what you are expected to do in a particular social situation.
zh: 而“the done thing”则是指在特定社交场合中被期待去做的事。

text: If you're miffed, you're slightly annoyed by someone's behaviour.
zh: 如果你感到miffed,就是说你因为某人的行为而有点生气。

text: And finally, a barney is a loud argument or quarrel.
zh: 最后,barney指的是激烈的争吵。

text: Once again, our six minutes are up, But if you're keen to practice these new words and phrases,
zh: 我们的六分钟又到时间了,不过如果你想多练习这些新单词和短语,

text: then check out our website, bbclearningenglish .com, where you'll find a worksheet and quiz for this episode.
zh: 欢迎访问我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com,那里有本期的练习题和小测验。

text: See you again soon, but for now, goodbye!
zh: 我们下次再见,现在先说再见啦!

text: Bye!
zh: 拜拜!
